title: Carved sycamore spatula-spoon
subtitle: A hybrid form for folding and scraping from green sycamore
excerpt: A sycamore spatula-spoon with a wide flat bowl and a squared front edge. Useful for folding batter, scraping down a jam pan, or stirring thick sauces. The form crosses the eating spoon with the flat spatula.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the kitchen-treen shaping method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the spatula-spoon form found in Victorian kitchens.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
A short vertical cut into the wood that stops the next push or pull cut from tearing the grain past the intended end-point.
A leather strap charged with abrasive compound, used to maintain a sharp edge between sharpenings.

content.text: The spatula-spoon is not quite a spoon and not quite a spatula. The bowl is wide and flat with a squared-off front edge rather than a rounded tip; the handle is longer than a standard eating spoon. It folds batters, scrapes the bottom of a preserving pan, and stirs thick porridges where a round-tipped spoon loses leverage. Sycamore is the recommended species: food-safe, pale, and the grain in
green
sycamore cuts cleanly across the bowl face.
Wood
Sycamore is the recommendation: the large interlocked grain takes cross-grain cuts without tearing, which matters on the wide flat bowl face. The starting
blank

should be 28 cm long and 8 cm wide to allow for the wider bowl. A half-round branch section or a riven slab both work. Birch is a close alternative; the bowl face will show more grain figure but carves just as cleanly.
Tools
Carving axe: for roughing the wide bowl area in plan.
Sloyd knife: for refining the handle, the flat bowl face, and the squared front edge.
Hook knife, medium (optional): for adding a slight hollow to the bowl face. The spatula-spoon works well flat, but some makers prefer a very shallow concavity of 2 to 3 mm.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Raw linseed oil for finishing.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Roughing the plan shape

The spatula-spoon is wider than a standard spoon blank at the bowl end. Mark the plan shape on the flat face: the bowl is 70 mm wide at its widest and 90 mm from tip to throat. The handle tapers from 20 mm at the throat to 14 mm at the tip. Axe the sides to the drawn line with controlled push cuts. The wider bowl means more material to remove from the sides; take three or four passes rather than trying to reach the line in one.
The flat bowl face

The defining feature of the spatula-spoon is the flat bowl face. With the flat face up, use push cuts to level the bowl surface. The blade travels from the throat end of the bowl toward the tip, with cuts parallel to the long axis. Sycamore's interlocked grain means that a cut going one way may take well while a cut reversed will catch a ray; test both directions and work with whichever cuts cleanly.
The squared front edge is formed with a
stop cut

across the grain at the tip of the bowl, then a push cut from the face down to the stop-cut line. This keeps the front edge sharp and square. The corner must be exactly perpendicular to the bowl face or the edge will drag along the pan bottom rather than scraping clean.
The handle and throat

The spatula-spoon handle is 18 cm long. Shape it to an oval cross-section: taller than it is wide so it sits comfortably in the hand for stirring pressure. Use thumb-pivot cuts to define the throat transition. Keep the throat 10 mm thick; the stirring forces on this form are higher than on an eating spoon.
Optional shallow hollow

If you want a slight hollow in the bowl face, the medium hook knife used very lightly will take 2 to 3 mm off the centre. Keep the hollow shallow and even; a deep hollow defeats the scraping purpose of the front edge. The flat version is the more traditional and more versatile form.
Finishing
Air-dry for two days, then apply raw linseed oil with a cloth, let soak for one hour, and wipe off surplus. Repeat after 24 hours. Two-week cure before food use. The
strop
can be used to polish the flat bowl face to a finer finish before oiling if you want a very smooth scraping surface.
Variations
Jam-pan version: 25 cm handle and a 100 mm wide bowl for reach into a deep preserving pan.
Slightly curved face: bend the blank slightly inward before it dries fully; the dried curve gives the bowl a gentle dish that holds sauces.
Left-handed: the squared edge works for both hands; only the hollow variant needs reversing.
Care
Hand-wash only, dry promptly. The wide flat bowl face is prone to absorbing water quickly; re-oil whenever it looks pale and dry.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The bowl face has a ridge along the centre
attrs.cause: Cuts from each direction met at the centreline but did not blend
attrs.fix: Use the sloyd knife in a slicing cut diagonally across the ridge to blend it. Work at about 45 degrees to the long axis for the blending cuts.

attrs.symptom: The squared front edge crumbled rather than cutting clean
attrs.cause: Stop cut not deep enough before the push cut from the face
attrs.fix: Set the stop cut first with a firm vertical stroke, 3 to 4 mm deep. Then come from the face with the push cut. The grain will break clean to the stop-cut line.

attrs.symptom: The bowl twisted as it dried
attrs.cause: Interlocked grain in the sycamore releasing tension on drying
attrs.fix: Sycamore occasionally twists. A slight twist in the bowl does not affect function. Dry slowly under light weight (a thick book on the bowl face) to reduce this.
